behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: This tests request construction at the function boundary. A real failure would be: the module calls the API but omits the taxonomy labels from the request. A Unit test that mocks the API client and inspects call_args can definitively catch this without needing any I/O. The mock captures exactly what arguments were passed, making this a pure verification of request assembly.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API client. Invoke the classification function with a sample document. Assert via mock.call_args that the API was called with request arguments containing the complete, unmodified taxonomy labels list.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns 'unknown' instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: This tests validation and fallback logic purely within the module. A real failure: the API returns 'spam' (not in taxonomy {legal, technical, financial, other}), and the module returns 'spam' to the caller instead of 'unknown'. A Unit test mocking the API to return invalid labels can verify this branching logic without any real I/O. The entire failure boundary is the module's label-validation code.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return a label not in the configured taxonomy (e.g., 'unexpected_type'). Invoke the classification function with a sample document. Assert that the function returns exactly 'unknown' rather than echoing the invalid label.

behaviors.description: The classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls.
behaviors.justification: This behavior likely requires the module to implement caching, since external LLM APIs are non-deterministic. A Unit test mocks the API to return a fixed label and calls the function twice with the same document, verifying either that the result is identical both times and/or that the API was called only once (proving caching). However, there's residual probability that the behavior intends to verify the API itself is deterministic, which would require a real API call (Agentic). The caching interpretation is more likely for a production system.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return a consistent label. Invoke the classification function twice with identical document and configuration. Assert that both calls return the same result. Assert that the API mock was called exactly once, proving the module caches results and avoids redundant API calls.

behaviors.description: The number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: A real failure is: real documents (various lengths, domains) exceed the token budget at the real API. A pure Unit test with arbitrary mocked token counts cannot catch this—it only verifies the enforcement logic (if tokens > budget, reject), not whether real documents actually exceed it. To truly validate this constraint, the test must either: (1) use real documents with a real tokenizer (Integration), or (2) hit the real API to measure actual token usage (System). A mock that always returns a low token count would pass even if real documents would blow the budget.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the classification function with a variety of 'typical' documents of varying lengths and domains. For each invocation, verify (via real tokenizer or realistic mock token counts) that the reported or measured token consumption does not exceed the configured per-call budget limit.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: This tests retry-and-backoff logic at the module boundary. A real failure: the API raises a rate-limit error, and the module propagates it immediately instead of retrying. A Unit test mocks the API to fail on the first call and succeed on the second, then verifies the final result is the success from the retry. The mock can also capture calls to a mocked sleep/delay function to verify the module actually waited. This covers all the branches (error detection, wait, retry, success) without needing real rate-limiting or network timing.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to raise a rate-limit error on the first invocation and return a valid classification on the second. Mock the sleep/wait function. Invoke the classification function. Assert that (a) the sleep function was called, (b) the API was called at least twice, and (c) the final result is the valid response from the successful retry, not the rate-limit error.
